literal: the inner controller; antar (within) + yāmin (controller, from √yam)
invariant_definition: The inner controller — the divine indwelling in every conscious and unconscious being, controlling from within. The locus classicus is the *Antaryāmi-Brāhmaṇa* (Bṛhadāraṇyaka 3.7), which describes Brahman as the inner controller of every element, every sense, and every soul. All Vedāntic schools accept the doctrine; they differ on whether the *antaryāmin* is identical with the *jīva*'s *ātman* (Advaita: yes, ultimately) or really distinct from it (Viśiṣṭādvaita: yes — Brahman is *antaryāmin* of the *jīva* who is His body; Dvaita: yes — Viṣṇu is *antaryāmin* of every *paratantra*).

per_school.definition: *Antaryāmin* is *Īśvara* — *saguṇa brahman* under the cosmic-control aspect. In *pāramārthika* analysis the *antaryāmin* is *brahman* and is identical with the *ātman* of the controlled.
*Antaryāmin* is Viṣṇu indwelling every *jīva* and every material object as their *śarīrī* (ensouler). The *antaryāmin*-*jīva* relation is the paradigm of *śarīra-śarīrī-bhāva*.
*Antaryāmin* is Viṣṇu indwelling every *paratantra* tattva, controlling from within while remaining eternally distinct.

translator_note: 'Inner controller' or 'indwelling Lord' both acceptable. The Christian-theological 'immanent God' captures part of it but loses the *control*-dimension.
